name: Tony Scott
abstract: Tony Scott (born Anthony Joseph Sciacca; June 17, 1921 – March 28, 2007) was an American jazz clarinetist, arranger, composer, bandleader, saxophonist and piano player with an interest in folk music around the world. For most of his career he was held in high esteem in new-age music circles because of his involvement in music linked to A…

concerts.label: Jazz Concert in Memory of Sidney Bechet

about_quotes.speaker: Red Norvo
about_quotes.evidence: I had a sextet with -- who'd I have? Dick Hyman and Della Lowe and Tony Scott, Joe Mangini, drummer and myself.

about_quotes.speaker: Art Farmer
about_quotes.evidence: Bill had been working with Tony, and Bill wrote a letter to the editor... That’s the way he was.
about_quotes.doc_title: Art Farmer — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Billy Taylor
about_quotes.evidence: My band included Doc Severson and Tony Scott and Jimmy Cleveland and Model Lowe and my trio... Our job was to play whatever was necessary to take jazz from it’s beginnings to what was then contemporary jazz.
about_quotes.doc_title: Billy Taylor — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: John Levy
about_quotes.evidence: The Bobby Tucker Quintet consisted of Denzil along with Mundell Lowe on guitar (Remo couldn't make it), Tony Scott on clarinet, Bobby and me.
about_quotes.doc_title: John Levy — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Evan Christopher
about_quotes.evidence: One of my other mentors was Tony Scott, who next to Buddy DeFranco was like the most significant clarinetist of the bebop period.
about_quotes.doc_title: Evan Christopher interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, October 1, 2009

about_quotes.speaker: Dick Katz
about_quotes.evidence: one of the first in quotes big time experiences I had was working at Minton’s Playhouse... with a clarinetist named Tony Scott.
about_quotes.doc_title: Dick Katz interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 7, 2002
